metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the canonical lock file path explicitly in the plan (e.g. `.github/release-requirements.txt`) and use that exact path in every AC reference, replacing `<lock>` with the concrete filename.
findings.finding: The lock file path is specified only as 'e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`', leaving the concrete path as a decision for the executor rather than a resolved plan choice. This causes AC3's proving command (`pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>`) to reference a placeholder `<lock>` with no canonical path, and means two developers could independently pick different locations.
findings.location: Scope section — 'A new release build lock/constraints file (e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`)'; AC3 — '`pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>`'
findings.suggested_fix: Choose and record one path — e.g. `.github/release-requirements.txt` — in the Scope section and replace all `<lock>` occurrences in the ACs with that path. Update AC7's `pip-compile` command to include the full invocation: `pip-compile --generate-hashes --output-file=.github/release-requirements.txt .github/release-requirements.in`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the corruption-test CI job to the Scope section as an explicit deliverable (naming the workflow file and trigger, e.g. a new `.github/workflows/test-mcp-verify.yml` triggered on PRs touching `release.yml`).
findings.finding: AC5's 'CI job that corrupts one byte of the pinned archive, runs the verify step, and asserts it exits non-zero' is described as a verification mechanism but is not listed in the Scope section as a file/workflow to create. A new CI job in an unnamed workflow file is a concrete deliverable that would be missed if an executor follows the scope bullets alone.
findings.location: AC5 — 'Verified by a CI job that corrupts one byte of the pinned archive…'; Scope section (no mention of a test workflow for the corruption check)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: '`.github/workflows/test-mcp-verify.yml` (new) — a PR-triggered workflow that downloads a test copy of the pinned archive, corrupts one byte, runs the `mcp_verify` sha256sum step, and asserts exit code is non-zero; triggered on changes to `release.yml`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `chmod +x mcp-publisher` step (or equivalent) in `mcp_registry` immediately after the artifact download, and note this in the plan's What section and AC5 to ensure the verified binary is executable before invocation.
findings.finding: The `mcp_verify` → `mcp_registry` artifact handoff will silently lose the `mcp-publisher` binary's executable bit: `actions/upload-artifact` v3+ does not preserve POSIX file permissions, so `mcp_registry`'s `./mcp-publisher login` call will fail with 'Permission denied' at runtime. The plan never mentions the need for a `chmod +x` step after downloading the artifact in `mcp_registry`.
findings.location: What section — 'Security contract — structural job split'; AC5 (mcp_registry job consumes the verified artifact)
findings.suggested_fix: In the `mcp_registry` job, after `actions/download-artifact` downloads the verified binary, add a step: `run: chmod +x mcp-publisher`. Document this in the plan's What section under the 'structural job split' description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a concrete multi-job evidence assembly strategy for `release-evidence.txt` that is compatible with GitHub Actions artifact semantics (e.g., each job writes a named sub-artifact; a final `assemble-evidence` job downloads all sub-artifacts and uploads a merged file, or a single job serializes all evidence writes after its predecessors complete via `needs:`).
findings.finding: The `release-evidence.txt` artifact is described as being written to ('appended') by multiple separate jobs/steps (SHA-pin lint, build step, mcp_verify job), but GitHub Actions artifacts are not appendable across job boundaries — uploading to the same artifact name from a later job overwrites rather than merges, making the evidence record potentially lossy and the ownership of the artifact's final state undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC for 'Release evidence records action SHAs, build-tool versions, and the MCP publisher version + verified digest'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the 'single artifact appended by multiple jobs' model with one of: (a) each contributing job uploads a distinct artifact (e.g., `lint-evidence.txt`, `build-evidence.txt`, `mcp-verify-evidence.txt`) and a final `assemble-evidence` job (needs: all prior jobs) downloads them all, concatenates them, and uploads `release-evidence.txt`; or (b) serialise all evidence-writing steps into a single job that runs last and has `needs:` on every other job, collecting outputs via job outputs or environment files. Document the chosen ownership model in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `chmod +x mcp-publisher` step in `mcp_registry` immediately after the artifact download (before `./mcp-publisher login github-oidc`), since `actions/upload-artifact` does not preserve the executable bit — document this in the plan's `mcp_registry` job spec.
findings.finding: `actions/upload-artifact` (v3+/v4) does NOT preserve POSIX file permissions — the executable bit on the extracted `mcp-publisher` binary will be lost when `mcp_verify` uploads it, so `mcp_registry`'s `./mcp-publisher login github-oidc` will fail with a permission-denied error unless a `chmod +x` step is added after download.
findings.location: What — MCP publisher section / AC5 (`mcp_verify` job uploads verified artifact; `mcp_registry` consumes it)
findings.suggested_fix: In the `mcp_registry` job spec, insert a step between the artifact download and the `login` step: `run: chmod +x mcp-publisher`. Alternatively, note in the plan that the `actions/upload-artifact`/`download-artifact` pair strips executable bits and that restoration is required.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC4's `--no-isolation` lint (e.g. `grep -q -- '--no-isolation' .github/workflows/release.yml`) so the check is independently verifiable.
findings.finding: AC4 states that 'a workflow lint/test step fails if `--no-isolation` is removed' but gives no concrete proving command for this lint, unlike AC1/AC2 (grep) and AC7 (grep). The mechanism — whether a grep, a Python script, or something else — is left to the executor, and the AC cannot be independently verified without knowing what command to run.
findings.location: AC4 — 'with a workflow lint/test step that fails if `--no-isolation` is removed (no free-resolution path remains)'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC4: '— verified by `grep -q -- "--no-isolation" .github/workflows/release.yml` (part of the same lint step as AC1/AC2, so a single lint script checks all three).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific artifact-validation tools to include in the lock file (or state 'none beyond `build`, Hatchling, and Twine' if that is the intent), removing the open-ended 'any artifact-validation tools' placeholder.
findings.finding: 'Artifact-validation tools' are listed as part of the lock file scope ('`build`, Hatchling, Twine, **any artifact-validation tools**, and their transitive deps') but are never named in the plan, leaving the executor to choose what belongs there.
findings.location: What section — 'Create a reviewed release lock/constraints file covering `build`, Hatchling, Twine, any artifact-validation tools, and their transitive deps'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'any artifact-validation tools' with the specific set, e.g.: '`build`, `hatchling`, `twine` (and their transitive runtime deps as produced by `pip-compile`); no additional validation tools at this time.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the `pip install` command in the 'What' body to include `--no-deps` (matching AC3), or explicitly note in both places whether `--no-deps` is required and why, so the implementer has a single authoritative command.
findings.finding: The 'What' body describes the hash-locked install as `pip install --require-hashes -r <lock>` (omitting `--no-deps`), but AC3 specifies `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>`. The `--no-deps` flag is load-bearing: without it, pip may satisfy unlisted transitive deps from the network, undermining the hash-lock guarantee. An implementer reading the body description will write a command that the AC's text then contradicts.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, change `pip install --require-hashes -r <lock>` to `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>` to match AC3 and add a parenthetical: '(--no-deps prevents pip from fetching unlisted transitive deps; the lock file must therefore be fully expanded via pip-compile --generate-hashes)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'What' section's `pip install` invocation to include `--no-deps` (matching AC3), and add a note explaining that `--no-deps` is required to prevent pip from silently installing unlocked transitive dependencies without hash verification.
findings.finding: The 'What' section specifies `pip install --require-hashes -r <lock>` (without `--no-deps`) for the hash-locked build-tool install, while AC3 correctly specifies `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>`. The omission of `--no-deps` in the 'What' section is a mechanism correctness gap: without `--no-deps`, pip may resolve additional transitive dependencies not present in the lock file and install them without hash verification, silently defeating the security guarantee that `--require-hashes` is intended to provide.
findings.location: What section — '...pre-install the hash-locked env (including Hatchling) with `pip install --require-hashes -r <lock>`'; AC3 — `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>`
findings.suggested_fix: Change the 'What' section text from `pip install --require-hashes -r <lock>` to `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>` and add a parenthetical: '(`--no-deps` prevents pip from resolving and installing transitive deps not in the lock file, which would bypass hash verification)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3/AC4) that actually runs `python -m build --no-isolation` with the locked env in CI on PRs and asserts a `.whl` file is produced (e.g. `ls dist/*.whl`), exercising the new build path end-to-end.
findings.finding: The `--no-isolation` build path is a new code path (replacing `pip install --upgrade build` + `python -m build`), but no AC exercises an actual end-to-end build run (i.e., running `python -m build --no-isolation` and verifying a wheel artifact is produced). AC3 verifies the install, AC4 verifies the flag is present in the workflow file, and AC8 runs `make test` — none of these run the actual build command against the locked env and confirm a valid wheel emerges.
findings.location: AC3, AC4, AC8; What section — 'pre-install the hash-locked env … then `python -m build --no-isolation`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4 or as a new AC: 'A PR-CI step runs `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt && python -m build --no-isolation` and asserts `ls dist/*.whl` exits 0 — verified on each PR touching `release.yml` or the lock file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether `zizmor` or `actionlint` (both purpose-built GitHub Actions security linters) can satisfy ACs 1 and 2 directly before committing to a bespoke grep-based lint step; if neither fits, extend `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` (the existing workflow-YAML assertion infrastructure) rather than creating a new CI artifact.
findings.finding: The plan mandates a 'new, self-contained workflow-lint/grep check' (ACs 1 and 2) to enforce SHA-pin and persist-credentials in release.yml, rebuilding (in a narrower, grep-only form) functionality that established tools like `zizmor` and `actionlint` already provide as maintained, audited implementations — without evaluating whether these tools could satisfy the ACs directly.
findings.location: AC1, AC2; 'What' section ('verified by a new, self-contained workflow-lint/grep check')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate replacing the bespoke grep-based workflow-lint (ACs 1 and 2) with `zizmor` (or `actionlint`) as the enforcement mechanism, and add a positive rationale in the plan for whichever linting mechanism is chosen — explaining why it is preferable on robustness, codebase-alignment, or simplicity grounds.
findings.finding: The plan mandates a bespoke 'new, self-contained workflow-lint/grep check' (ACs 1 and 2) to detect mutable action references and absent `persist-credentials: false` in `release.yml`. This reinvents functionality already provided by `zizmor` — a dedicated, actively-maintained GitHub Actions security linter that detects unpinned actions, overly broad permissions, and missing `persist-credentials`. A bespoke grep check is structurally less robust (misses composite-action `uses:`, `workflow_call` references, inline expressions, and future patterns) and creates an ongoing maintenance burden. The plan provides no positive rationale for choosing bespoke grep over `zizmor`/`actionlint`.
findings.location: AC1 — 'verified by a new, self-contained workflow-lint/grep check'; AC2 — 'verified by the same workflow-lint step'; What section — 'this story adds a **new, self-contained** SHA-pin lint'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the bespoke grep-based workflow-lint check vs. adopting `zizmor` (or `actionlint`) for AC1/AC2 enforcement — or replace the custom check with a `zizmor --pedantic release.yml` invocation that natively detects unpinned `uses:` and missing `persist-credentials`, scoped to `release.yml`, with a rationale comment for why the established tool is preferred or not.
findings.finding: The plan mandates 'a new, self-contained workflow-lint/grep check' (ACs 1 and 2) to enforce SHA-pin and persist-credentials constraints in release.yml, but established tools — `zizmor` (a dedicated GitHub Actions security linter written in Rust, with rules for pinned-actions and missing persist-credentials) and `actionlint` — already solve this problem with maintained rule sets and structured output. The plan does not mention either tool, does not justify why bespoke grep is preferred over them, and introduces maintenance burden (the custom check itself becomes a supply-chain artifact that can drift).
findings.location: What section ('a new, self-contained workflow-lint/grep check'); AC1; AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the planned bespoke grep check with `zizmor --format sarif --pedantic .github/workflows/release.yml` (or `actionlint -oneline .github/workflows/release.yml`) as the AC1/AC2 verifier. Add `zizmor` as a pinned dev-tool entry (with its own SHA-256 in the lock file) and update the AC language to reference the chosen tool instead of 'a new, self-contained workflow-lint/grep check'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve at plan time whether `modelcontextprotocol/registry` releases ship a `checksums.txt` (or equivalent provenance artifact) for the `mcp-publisher` binary, and commit to one concrete verification approach in the plan rather than a conditional 'if upstream ships one'.
findings.finding: The plan conditionally defers verification of whether the MCP registry upstream ships a `checksums.txt` — 'Prefer an upstream `checksums.txt` if `modelcontextprotocol/registry` ships one; otherwise hard-code the SHA-256 computed at pin time' — without resolving this at plan time. The `modelcontextprotocol/registry` is a relatively new project and the plan leaves the implementer to discover at implementation time whether official checksums exist, creating an unverified capability assertion that affects the security contract (verified digest vs. self-computed hard-code).
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher subsection ('Prefer an upstream `checksums.txt` if `modelcontextprotocol/registry` ships one').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit, minimal `permissions:` block to the `mcp_registry` job in the plan's spec (e.g., `id-token: write` only, with all other permissions absent or set to `none`/`read`), and require the same for `mcp_verify` (which needs no elevated permissions during internet download).
findings.finding: The `mcp_registry` job's `permissions` block is undeclared beyond `id-token: write`. A release workflow typically holds `contents: write` (to create GitHub Releases) and possibly other elevated grants at the workflow level; without an explicit job-level `permissions` block that names ONLY the minimum required (e.g., `id-token: write` alone), `mcp_registry` inherits whatever the workflow-level default carries. Because this job downloads no binary and only runs `mcp-publisher login`/`publish`, any inherited `contents: write` or other grants are excess privilege on the OIDC-minting boundary.
findings.location: What / MCP publisher section — 'mcp_registry' job definition; AC: 'mcp_verify job has no id-token: write; mcp_registry job needs: mcp_verify'
findings.suggested_fix:
In the plan's `mcp_registry` job spec, add:
```yaml
permissions:
  id-token: write   # OIDC for MCP registry publish — only grant needed
```
and for `mcp_verify`:
```yaml
permissions: {}    # no privileges needed during download + verify
```
Require in the AC that a lint step asserts no other permissions appear on these two jobs.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
